explanation_md: `p` points at the first character of the literal "Coder". `<<` on a `const char*` prints all characters until `'\0'`, so the first line is `Coder`. `*p` dereferences to `'C'`, the first character. `*(p + 2)` advances two positions past 'C' and 'o', landing on `'d'`.

prompt_md: Why must you write `const char* msg = "Hello";` rather than `char* msg = "Hello";` in C++?

options.a: Because `char*` is not large enough to hold a string
options.b: Because string literals are stored in read-only memory and must not be modified through the pointer
options.c: Because `const char*` automatically appends the null terminator
options.d: Because `char*` requires a cast before it can hold any address

explanation_md: String literals reside in read-only (static) memory. Assigning the address to a non-`const` pointer creates the false impression that you can modify those bytes. Writing through such a pointer is undefined behavior and will likely crash. `const char*` makes the read-only nature explicit. Option (a), (c), and (d) are all incorrect — the pointer width and the null terminator are unrelated to the `const` qualifier.

options.a: The program uses `const char*` instead of `char[]`
options.b: `==` compares pointer addresses, not the string contents, so the result is implementation-defined
options.c: You cannot initialize two `const char*` variables with the same string literal
options.d: The `if` condition should use `!= nullptr` before comparing

explanation_md: `==` on two `const char*` values compares the stored addresses, not the characters they point to. Some compilers merge identical literals so `a == b` is true, but this is not guaranteed by the standard. To compare string contents you need `std::strcmp` from `<cstring>`, or use `std::string` which overloads `==` for content comparison.

prompt_md: What is stored in each element of the array `const char* colors[] = {"red", "green", "blue"};`?

options.a: The actual characters of each color word, null-terminated, inline in the array
options.b: A pointer to the first character of each string literal
options.c: The integer ASCII values of the first character of each word

explanation_md: An array of `const char*` stores pointers, one per element. Each pointer points to the first character of the corresponding string literal in read-only program memory. The array itself contains only pointer values (typically 4 or 8 bytes each), not the characters themselves.

`words[i]` is a `const char*` pointing to the i-th string literal. Applying `[0]` to it gives the first character of that literal. So `words[0][0]` is `'c'`, `words[1][0]` is `'d'`, and `words[2][0]` is `'b'`.
